next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

By the time the layover meal opened at Caldera Junction, the cafeteria smelled of fryer grease, wet denim, and the thin ammoniac sweetness that lifted off a crew after a full intake day. Marisol carried her tray with both hands because her left knee had gone wooden on the metal stairs and because the stew trembled in its compartment like something barely set. The windows facing the commuter platform were filmed white from old detergent. Beyond them, on the maintenance track, the deportation cars sat in their patient row with the white paint failing at the seams. On the other side of the glass a commuter train slid in clean and silver, doors opening for people who still got to decide where they were going.

She took the corner table by the dead vending machine because it gave her a view of both doors. That was habit, not paranoia. Or at least it was not only habit. Her hair was pinned so tight the skin at her temples ached. Bleach had dried the backs of her hands into a fine white nap, except over the old burn on her right wrist, where the skin never roughened evenly again. She set down the tray, sat carefully, and listened to the room: forks on plastic, boots dragging, a cook cursing in the steam line, the constant low vibration from the yard under all of it. The whole junction seemed built to remind a body that metal did not rest merely because people did.

Paloma arrived first, compact and square in her scrubs under an unbuttoned work shirt, silver braid darkened with sweat down her back. She carried her own tray in one hand and two little rinse cups in the o...
open_hooks_to_carry: Get Gabriel into the trauma room through a plausible work pretext.
Make his bruises and confiscated recorder materially present.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ain was becoming a fact, and it was her fact, built this morning by signatures, wipes, thresholds, bags changed in time, straps dried enough to pass inspection, one manual bind corrected instead of challenged.

Her slate buzzed with the departure matrix: Southbound Meridian West, Puerta Seco release 7:40, status `ON SCHEDULE`.

She stood with the ballast crunching under her shoes and looked through the narrow reinforced slit in Car 8's wall. She could not see Gabriel from there, only moving shadows, the blur of a child's hand, one face turning and gone.

The duplicate case sat in local cache under a false sanitation code. The fresh band on Gabriel's wrist said Rafael Avelar. Tom Avelar's dead certificate was doing live work in the system. Teresa had warned against preventable holds. The blackwater alarm might still climb if the heat kept up. The trauma room might fill again before noon. The entire line was sliding toward departure on the rails Marisol knew better than any room she had ever called home.

The worst part was that none of the train's numbers cared.

Another horn sounded. The board dropped to final two-minute.

Marisol touched the passcard clipped to her breast pocket as if the plastic might tell her which part of the morning still belonged to her. It was slick with sweat. She had spent half her life learning how to keep this corridor dispatchable, how to make blood into paperwork and waste into thresholds and panic into a schedule nobody above her had to smell.

Now her son was inside the cleaned car under another man's country, and the train was still on time.
state_deltas_required: Locked receiving-state paperwork, guarded medical access, and years of estrangement compacted into minutes.
Gabriel rejects quiet rescue, names Marisol as part of the line's erasure, and turns an old family wound into active resistance.
